coredata.dc:title: Comparative studies on the combustion characteristics of electrolytes and carbonate mixed solvents with flame retardant additives under low pressures
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Qiurui
Weng, Jingwen
Ouyang, Dongxu
Chen, Mingyi
Wang, Xuehui
Wang, Jian
coredata.dc:description: Adding flame retardant additives is an effective approach to improve the safety of lithium-ion battery electrolytes. This study discusses the inhibiting effects of three flame retardant additives on the combustion characteristics of electrolyte and carbonate mixed solvent under low pressure. The experimental results show that the inhibition of flame retardant additives mainly occurs in the low-boiling carbonate control stage. In contrast, the combustion of the high-boiling carbonate control stage is promoted, and the promotion effect is more significant under low pressure. When the pressure is 40 kPa, it is difficult to maintain the combustion of the high-boiling point carbonate control stage of carbonate mixed solvent, but the carbonate mixed solvent containing phenoxycyclophosphazene. Phenoxycyclophosphazene has a better flame retardant effect on the mass loss rate and combustion time of carbonate mixed solvent compared with other additives. Due to the flame retardant effect of lithium salt, the electrolyte can only complete the combustion of the low-boiling point carbonate control stage, and the flame retardant effects of the flame retardant additives in the electrolyte on the combustion mass, mass loss rate, and combustion time are weakened as the pressure decreases. Tris (2-chloropropyl)phosphate assists in reducing the temperature of the electrolyte and carbonate mixed solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Electrolyte
Flame retardant additive
Low pressure
Fire hazards

The MLR curves of carbonate mixed solvent during tests.
Average MLR curves of two stages of carbonate mixed solvent.
Solvent temperature curves: (a) electrolyte; (b) carbonate mixture solvent.
The flame of carbonate mixed solvents and electrolytes in the stable combustion stage.
Thermochemical and physical properties of EC, DMC, and EMC [29].
Physical and chemical properties of the three flame retardant additives [30].

head.ce:title: Comparative studies on the combustion characteristics of electrolytes and carbonate mixed solvents with flame retardant additives under low pressures

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Adding flame retardant additives is an effective approach to improve the safety of lithium-ion battery electrolytes. This study discusses the inhibiting effects of three flame retardant additives on the combustion characteristics of electrolyte and carbonate mixed solvent under low pressure. The experimental results show that the inhibition of flame retardant additives mainly occurs in the low-boiling carbonate control stage. In contrast, the combustion of the high-boiling carbonate control stage is promoted, and the promotion effect is more significant under low pressure. When the pressure is 40 kPa, it is difficult to maintain the combustion of the high-boiling point carbonate control stage of carbonate mixed solvent, but the carbonate mixed solvent containing phenoxycyclophosphazene. Phenoxycyclophosphazene has a better flame retardant effect on the mass loss rate and combustion time of carbonate mixed solvent compared with other additives. Due to the flame retardant effect of lithium salt, the electrolyte can only complete the combustion of the low-boiling point carbonate control stage, and the flame retardant effects of the flame retardant additives in the electrolyte on the combustion mass, mass loss rate, and combustion time are weakened as the pressure decreases. Tris (2-chloropropyl)phosphate assists in reducing the temperature of the electrolyte and carbonate mixed solution.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Facing the depletion of fossil fuels and the environmental degradation caused by excessive carbon emissions, lithium-ion battery (LIB) is a promising energy-storage solution due to its high energy density, long cycle life, and limited pollution [1,2]. Since commercialized in 1991, LIBs not only have been widely used in portable devices, electric vehicles, and energy storage systems under atmospheric pressure, but also in numerous low-pressure environments like unmanned aerial vehicles (UAV), aircraft, satellites, and the plateau [3–5]. Due to the widespread application in the industrial and military, battery thermal hazards occur more frequently, resulting in significant loss of lives and properties. Hence, the battery thermal safety issue has become a heated topic [6,7].

The process of battery thermal runaway is a complicated and violent behavior consisting of a series of chemical reactions, such as the decomposition of the electrolyte, lithium salt, and cathode and the interaction among these components. These violent chemical exothermic reactions can release a great amount of combustible particles and gases, accompanied by sparks, fires, and even explosions [8–11]. In addition, there is a special pattern for the thermal runaway of LIBs under low pressure. As the pressure decreases, the average mass loss rate (MLR), surface temperature, and peak flame temperature of the battery decrease [3], the thermal runaway propagation rate increases and then decreases [12], and the safety venting is earlier and stronger [13]. In addition, factors such as the airflow rate, inert gas atmosphere, and electrical connection mode will have unique effects on the thermal runaway of LIBs under low-pressure environment [5,12,14]. Thermal runaway of LIBs under low pressure deserves more attention.

To enhance battery thermal safety from the perspective of intrinsic safety, it is important to investigate the thermal behaviors of each component of a battery. The electrolyte is regarded as the most typical combustible component with little mass (c.a. 11%) but a large amount of heat release (c.a. 47.6%) [15] during thermal runaway of the LIB. On the other hand, the spray of electrolyte is an important reason for the flame spreading in battery fire [16].

Regarding the safety of electrolytes, there have been numerous studies concerning the thermal stability of electrolytes using C80 calorimeter [17], thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) [18], accelerated adiabatic calorimeter [19,20], differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) [21,22], and other means. Investigation of its combustion behaviors is still rare and the existing research gap can result in a lack of comprehensive evaluation of the thermal safety of electrolyte. The following studies on the combustion characteristics of electrolytes have been carried out. Eshetu et al. [23] used fire propagation apparatus to study the combustion characteristics of eight kinds of standard electrolytes and found that the enthalpy of combustion was related to the solvent mixture and the toxic products were dependent on the added lithium salt. Harris et al. [24] studied the thermochemical and combustion characteristics of the gases released during the combustion of carbonate mixed solvents and found significant differences in the combustion enthalpy and evaporation enthalpy of carbonate mixed solvents. Zhang et al. [25] investigated the heat release rate (HRR) and mass loss rate of mixtures of ethyl carbonate (EC)/dimethyl carbonate (DMC)/diethyl carbonate (DEC) using an ISO 5660 cone calorimeter and compared the HRR of the oxygen consumption method and the stoichiometric method, which were in agreement. Fu et al. [26] measured the HRR, MLR, flame height, and gas release data of the electrolyte with different external heat fluxes and different sizes of oil pans under a cone calorimeter, and established a peak HRR empirical model for electrolyte pool fire based on pan diameter and external heat flux. Ding et al. [27] studied the effect of LiPF6 on electrolyte combustion, divided the combustion process into three stages by carbonates with different boiling points that dominated the combustion, and predicted the boiling points of binary carbonate solvents based on phase equilibrium theory. Chen et al. [28–31] compared the combustion characteristics of four carbonates, EC, ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC), DMC, and DEC, and their binary, ternary carbonate mixed solvents with 0 kW/m2 and 30 kW/m2 thermal radiation. EC was found to decrease the peak HRR, and external radiation significantly increased both the peak HRR and MLR. They explored the effects of adding separator and lithium salt into ternary carbonate mixed solvent. The separator increased the fire hazard, and the lithium salt continued to absorb heat and had the opposite effect. In addition, the combustion rates of carbonate mixed solvents containing three flame retardant additives were tested respectively, and they all decreased to different degrees. Liu et al. [32–34] compared and analyzed the effects of LiBF4, LiPF6, and different carbonate mixing ratios on the combustion characteristics of electrolytes at three different altitudes. At higher altitude, the total heat release was smaller and both the average and peak HRR were significantly reduced, while the burn duration was significantly increased. The addition of LiBF4 could not effectively improve the safety of the electrolyte, and the increase in the proportion of propylene carbonate will have a lower fire hazard and overall performance. Huang et al. [35] found that the DEC-containing electrolyte had a higher HRR. They compared the HRR of the DEC-containing electrolyte under the thermochemical method and the oxygen consumption method and found that the two were not consistent, which was different from the conclusion reported in the previous study. Through experiments, it was found that the fire point and the open cup flash point temperature of the electrolyte are very close. In summary, the existing studies on the combustion characteristics of electrolytes include the following aspects. The initial relevant studies were on the enthalpy of combustion and combustion products of electrolytes. A large part of the subsequent studies focused on experimental studies related to the combustion of electrolytes using cone calorimeter, with the main variables studied including external radiation, oil pan size, carbonate composition ratio, lithium salt, and carbonate type. For the conical calorimeter measurements, some researchers have also compared the differences between the results and those calculated by thermochemical methods. A few studies have also involved the combustion characteristics of electrolytes at three altitudes and the combustion rates of electrolytes containing three flame retardant additives.

One of the most effective approaches to enhance electrolyte safety is to add flame retardant additives. Various types of flame retardant additives have been developed, and flame retardant additives can be classified according to the elements that produce the flame retardant effect: phosphorus-based flame retardant additives [36–38], halogen-based flame retardant additives [39,40] and composite flame retardants [41–43]. However, the addition of flame retardant additive cause some negative effects, such as increasing the viscosity of electrolyte [44,45], affecting the formation of SEI film, destroying the graphite structure [46,47], reducing the conductivity of the electrolyte [48,49], and affecting the battery capacity [40], etc. The exploration of figuring out a safer electrolyte with less sacrifice on the electrochemical performance of the LIB is still worth trying.

Despite the above works, there are few studies on the combustion characteristics of electrolytes containing flame retardant additives, and most of the previous work was carried out under standard atmospheric pressure. In low-pressure environments, the combustion behaviors of electrolytes and the flame retarding characteristics of flame retardant additives also urgently need to be further understood. This extended study is expected to explore whether the known thermal behaviors and flame retardant effects are also applicable under low pressures.

In this work, phosphorus-based flame retardant additive and composite flame retardant are selected, conducted a series of burning experiments under atmospheric pressure and low-pressure environments. The decomposition of LiPF6 will inevitably affect the combustion of the electrolyte [30], so we set up electrolytes and carbonate mixed solvents without lithium salt as a comparative study. The comparison of the MLR, flame shape, and fuel temperature during the experiments are conducted qualitatively and quantitatively. We anticipate that the interesting findings of this work can provide a more comprehensive evaluation of electrolyte thermal safety and provide some reference as well as guidance for the safety design of lithium-ion battery electrolytes, especially under low pressures.

The samples used in this paper were commercial LB10 standard electrolyte and carbonate mixed solvent (Suzhou Ganmin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd). LB10 electrolyte is made of 1.0 mol/L LiPF6 dissolved in EC, DMC, and EMC 1:1:1 (mass ratio) carbonate solvent. The carbonate mixed solvent consists of EC, DMC, and EMC with a mass ratio of 1:1:1. The physical and thermochemical parameters of the three organocarbonates are shown in Table 1 .

Three flame retardant additives were used in this paper, namely triphenyl phosphate (TPP), phenoxycyclophosphazene (HPCTP), and tris(clorisopropyl)phosphate (TCPP). All three additives are from Aladdin Reagents Ltd. TPP is one of the most commercially available phosphorus-based flame retardants [50] and contains a flame retardant element P. HPCTP is a phosphonitrile flame retardant additive with two flame retardant elements, P and N. Phosphoronitrile flame retardant additives generally have good electrochemical properties [51]. TCPP contains two flame retardant elements, P and Cl, and is readily soluble in carbonate solvents. The physical and chemical properties of the three flame retardant additives are listed in Table 2 .

As shown in Fig. 1 , the experiments were conducted in a variable-pressure combustion chamber with a length of 3 m, a width of 2 m, and a height of 2 m, and the variable pressure ranged from 15 to 100 kPa. In this paper, a digital camera with 25 fps (SONY XPS160) was used to record the combustion process outside the observation window. The sample container was a stainless steel oil pan with a diameter of 0.08 m and a depth of 0.02 m. The change of sample mass loss was recorded using an electronic balance (Mettler Toledo XP10002S) with a resolution of 0.01 g and a maximum range of 9 kg. The temperature of the electrolyte in the oil pan was measured using a K-type thermocouple (ShangHai BoXiang Galvanothermy Instrument Co., Ltd) with a diameter of 1 mm and a maximum range of 1100 K, which is placed 1 mm high near the bottom of the oil pan and 5 mm from the edge.

In this experiment, the oil pan containing the sample was placed inside the combustion chamber. The pressure control device started off until the pressure dropped to the target pressure, and a high-temperature resistance wire was used to ignite the sample. At any pressure, the control resistance wire ignited the sample within 5 s of energizing, and the sample evaporated within 0.1 g before igniting, which was considered to be of constant mass. The environment of the chamber would be refreshed with air at the end of each test.

For the three flame retardant additives, four groups of experiments including a blank control group without flame retardant additive were set up. Each experimental group contained two sample types, namely the electrolyte with lithium salt in it and carbonate mixed solvent without lithium salt in it, in order to investigate the effect of lithium salt on combustion.

34.26 g of electrolyte contains 30 g of carbonate mixed solvents and 4.26 g of LiPF6. The two samples for each experimental group were tested at four pressures of 100 kPa, 80 kPa, 60 kPa, and 40 kPa, respectively. The addition of flame retardant additive in electrolyte generally does not exceed 20% [52], and the addition of all three flame retardant additives in this experiment was 10% of carbonate mixed solvent, 3.33 g. Details are listed in Table 3 .

As displayed in Table 4 , the combustion masses of carbonate mixed solvents are approximately the mass of all carbonates 30 g. While the combustion masses of electrolytes are approximately two-thirds of carbonate solvent 20 g, and the combustion residue is also darker and thicker than the former. The effect of flame retardant additives on the combustion masses of the two kinds of samples differed with decreasing pressure. In particular, the combustion masses of the four carbonate mixed solvents at 40 kPa show a large difference. The combustion residue and combustion mass are further analyzed by combining the combustion mechanism of carbonate and the retarding mechanism of flame retardant additive.

The combustion process of carbonate solvent can be described by formulas (1)-(4) [36]. The carbonate solvent changes from liquid to gaseous when heated by flame and decomposes to produce H·, and H· react with oxygen to form HO· and O·. Hydrogen may be derived from the decomposition of electrolytes and existing trace water. HO· and O· will react with hydrogen to make more H·. The free radicals in the above reactions have strong reactivity, along with a large amount of heat generation, and play an important role in maintaining combustion.
(1)
R H
→
f l a m e
R ∙ + H ∙
(2)
H ∙ +
O 2
→ H O ∙ + O ∙
(3)
H O ∙ +
H 2
→ H ∙ +
H 2
O
(4)
O ∙ +
H 2
→ H O ∙ + H ∙

LiPF6 in the electrolyte during combustion is endothermically decomposed to produce LiF and PF5. The main decomposition process is shown in formula (5).
(5)
L i P
F 6
→ L i F ↓ + P
F 5
↑

As an inorganic salt, LiF partially isolates the combustible carbonate solvent from oxygen in the liquid phase, while PF5 is released into the flame zone and inhibits the flame in a complex chemical reaction with O2, H2O, and carbonate vapor [53]. LiPF6 acts as a flame retardant in both ways. The difference between the electrolyte without flame retardant additive and the carbonate mixed solvents with three flame retardants added respectively is only that the flame retardant additive or LiPF6 is added. By comparing the data between the two in Table 4, except for 40 kPa, the combustion mass of the electrolyte containing LiPF6 is nearly 20 g much less than the burning mass of the carbonate mixed solution of about 30 g, which shows that the LiPF6 itself has better flame retardant performance than the three flame retardant additives.

In current studies, retarding mechanisms of flame retardant additives are mainly divided into condensed-phase flame retardant and gas-phase flame retardant [54], and the following describes the phosphorus-based flame retardant as an example. Gas-phase flame retardancy can be explained by the free radical mechanism. The phosphorus-based flame retardants decompose at high temperatures to produce gaseous free radicals PO·, which can capture H· and HO· so that the concentration of H· and HO· in the combustion region decrease and it is more difficult to maintain combustion. This process can be expressed by formulas (6)-(9).
(6)
P F R
l i q u i d
→
h e a t
P F R
g a s
(7)
P F R
g a s
→
f l a m e
PO ∙
(8)
P O ∙ + H ∙ → H P O
(9)
P O ∙ + H O ∙ → H P O + O

The flame retardancy process in the condensed phase can be expressed by formulas (10)-(13). The flame retardant containing phosphorous is decomposed into phosphorous oxygen-containing acid when heated, which catalyzes the endothermic dehydration of hydroxyl compounds into carbon reaction to generate water and char. The char layer is difficult to ignite and has the function of heat insulation and oxygen isolation to slow down the thermal decomposition of the base material. In addition, the water vapor formed by dehydration can dilute the concentration of oxygen and combustible gas in the gas phase [55]. To study the possible reaction between flame retardant additives and electrolyte or carbonate solvent and the mechanism of flame retardancy, it is necessary to further study the composition of the residues.
(10)
C 3
H 4
O 3
(
E C
)
→
H
3
P
O
4
3 C + 2
H 2
O + O
(11)
C 3
H 6
O 3
(
DM C
)
→
H
3
P
O
4
3 C + 3
H 2
O
(12)
C 4
H 8
O 3
(
E M C
)
→
H
3
P
O
4
4 C + 3
H 2
O + 2 H ·
(13)
2 H · + O →
H 2
O

Further in-depth analysis is performed on the three kinds of carbonate in the carbonate mixed solvent and electrolyte. The vaporization rates of linear carbonate DMC and EMC reached the maximum value at 100 °C, while cyclic carbonate EC reached the maximum value at 230 °C [56]. Table 1 shows that the boiling point and heat of vaporization of cyclic carbonate EC are nearly twice those of linear carbonate, and its flashpoint is much higher than that of linear carbonate. This suggests that the EC takes more heat to evaporate and less heat to burn. Based on the above analysis, we can speculate that the thermal stability of DMC and EMC is far less than that of EC. DMC and EMC more easily evaporate and burn, so they are mainly consumed in the early stage of combustion, while EC is mainly consumed in the late stage.

In combination with the above analysis and combustion mass in Table 4, it can be concluded that the combustion of the electrolyte mainly consumes the low-boiling carbonates, while the endothermic decomposition of LiPF6 and its decomposition products prevent the combustion of most high-boiling carbonates. The combustion mass with the addition of flame retardant additives is less than that without flame retardant additives. At 100 kPa, the effect of flame retardant additives on reducing combustion mass is the most significant, and the effect on reducing combustion mass becomes weaker with decreasing pressure. At 40 kPa, the combustion masses of the three kinds of mixed carbonate solvents containing flame retardant additives are the closest to that of the solvent without additives. The reduction of combustion mass caused by TPP is most affected by pressure, and the decreased combustion mass as a percentage of the combustion mass of the without additive group under the same pressure decreases from 8.3% of 100 kPa to 0.7% of 40 kPa. For TCPP, which is least affected by pressure, the percentage reduction in combustion mass decreases from 7.3% at 100 kPa to 2.4% at 40 kPa.

As shown in Table 4, in the range of 60–100 kPa, the combustion masses of the mixed carbonate solvents with flame retardant additives do not change much. The largest reduction in combustion mass occurs in HPCTP at 100 kPa, which decreases by 2.9% compared with no addition. Notably, at 40 kPa, the combustion masses of carbonate mixed solvent without additive and carbonate mixed solvent with TPP and TCPP are 20.83 g, 21.5 g, and 20.85 g, respectively, which significantly decrease compared with the combustion mass under higher pressure. Combining the above analysis and the combustion mass in Table 4, it can be concluded that there is also a lot of high-boiling carbonate EC that does not burn. However, the combustion mass of the HPCTP carbonate mixture solvent at 40 kPa reaches 29.86 g, where the high-boiling point carbonate EC also undergoes near-complete combustion, which becomes more dangerous for the carbonate mixed solvent at extremely low pressure. This phenomenon will be further explained in section 3.2 in conjunction with MLR.

It can be seen from Fig. 3 that when the pressure is in the range of 60 kPa–100 kPa, after the ignition of carbonate mixed solvent, the MLR rises rapidly and enters the stable combustion stage. After the first peak appears, the MLR decreases, followed by an inflection point, then increases, and the second peak appears, followed by a rapid decline, and enters the extinction stage. According to the analysis of the three kinds of combustible carbonates in section 3.1 and the research of Ding [27], taking the MLR of the four carbonate mixed solvents at 100 kPa in Fig. 3 as examples, the stable combustion stage can be divided into two parts from the inflection point, corresponding to stage 1 contributed by the low-boiling point carbonates EMC and DMC, and stage 2 contributed by the high-boiling point carbonates EC. As the pressure decreases, the MLR of the four carbonate mixed solvents decreases accordingly. MLR (
m ˙
″
) can be expressed as formula (14) [57]:
(14)
m ˙
″
=
Q ˙
a ″
−
Q ˙
l ″
H v
where
H v
is the latent heat of vaporization of the fuel,
Q ˙
a ″
is the heat feedback from the flame to the liquid surface, and
Q ˙
l ″
is the heat loss. The heat feedback of a flame to a liquid pool through various pathways, which include conduction, convection, and radiation, is expressed in formula (15).
(15)
Q ˙
a ″
=
q ˙
c o n d
″
+
q ˙
c o n v
″
+
q ˙
r a d
″

According to previous studies [58], under lower pressure, all three types of heat transfer will be reduced, including the convection-dominated 0.08 m oil pan [59] used in this article. The heat loss
Q ˙
l ″
in the oil pool fire is mainly based on the re-radiation feedback of the liquid to the environment, and the re-radiation energy is extremely small, which can be simplified. As the pressure decreases, the saturation temperature of the fuel decreases, and the kinetic energy of the liquid decreases, so the
H v
increases. The change of the above-mentioned parameters under low pressure is also a sign of the decline of the MLR. As the pressure decreases, the MLR decreases, and the heat generated by combustion decreases. When the heat fed back to the liquid surface is too small, it is not enough to continue to burn.

Compared with general hydrocarbon fuels such as propane, butane, etc., carbonates have lower combustion heat and higher vaporization heat, and EC is the most significant of the above-mentioned effects among carbonates commonly used in electrolytes [24]. Therefore, at 40 kPa, in addition to the carbonate mixed solvent containing HPCTP, the other three carbonate mixed solvents can only complete the combustion of stage 1, so the combustion masses are also greatly reduced (see Table 4). It can be inferred that there is a critical pressure between 40 and 60 kPa that determines whether the high-boiling point EC can continue to burn and the safety of carbonate mixed solvent is greatly improved below the critical pressure.

The average MLR during the two stages of stable combustion MLR is shown in Fig. 4 . At 100 kPa, the average MLR of stage 1 dominated by the low-boiling point carbonate is much larger than that of stage 2 dominated by the high-boiling point carbonate because the low-boiling point carbonate evaporates more easily. As the pressure decreases, the gap of average MLR between stages 1 and 2 gradually narrows, and the transition between them gradually smooths out. The inhibition of the three flame retardant additives on the average MLR of the carbonate mixed solvent is mainly in stage 1, and the largest inhibition is demonstrated by TPP at 40 kPa, which decreases by 7.0% compared with that without the additive, followed by HPCTP at 40 kPa, which decreases by 4.9%. In stage 2, the average MLR is increased by flame retardant additives, with the largest increase in HPCTP at 80 kPa (15.4%).

According to the average MLR of the two stages of carbonate mixed solvent, the flame retardant effect of the three flame retardant additives is mainly concentrated in stage 1. For low-boiling point carbonates which are easy to vaporize, the flame retardant additives inhibit the combustion, and HPCTP has the most obvious inhibition effect on the average MLR. The average MLRs of stage 2 are significantly higher than those of the carbonate mixed solvents without additives. Between 60 kPa and 100 kPa, HPCTP also has a more significant promoting effect on the average MLR. At 40 kPa, the high-boiling point carbonate, which can not burn, continues to burn under the promotion of HPCTP. The inhibition of low-boiling carbonate and the promotion of high-boiling carbonate are also observed in combustion experiments on carbonate mixed solvents containing tributyl phosphate (TBP), hexamethyl phosphorotriamine (HMPA), and hexafluorocyclotriphosphazenecarbonitrile (HFPN) [60]. The reason why flame retardants can promote the combustion of high-boiling carbonate may be that the active free radicals generated by the decomposition of flame retardants can induce the accelerated decomposition of hard-to-decompose high-boiling carbonate [61]. To determine the cause of this phenomenon, our next step will be to conduct an in-depth study of the combustion process products and residues.

Table 5 shows the combustion times of the electrolyte and carbonate mixed solvent at various pressures. In general, all three flame retardant additives can shorten the combustion time of the electrolyte. As the pressure decreases, the effect of the three flame retardant additives reducing the combustion time also decreases gradually.

For the carbonate mixed solvent, from 60 to 100 kPa, the flame retardant additives can effectively reduce the combustion time, which is similar to the phenomenon in the electrolyte. Among them, HPCTP shows a special pattern, and the combustion time of electrolyte after adding HPCTP is shortened by 10.1% and 9.5% at 100 and 80 kPa, respectively, compared with that without additive, which is much larger than that of the other two flame retardant additives, but exceeds the combustion time of the other two flame retardant additives at 60 kPa instead, which is close to the combustion time without adding flame retardant additives. At 40 kPa, the combustion time is greatly increased due to the combustion of stage 2, which is much longer than that of the other three carbonate mixed solvents. Combined with the analysis of MLR and combustion time, HPCTP under lower pressure does not improve the safety of carbonate mixed solvents but makes them more dangerous.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis on solvent temperature and flame
ce:section.ce:para: As the pressure decreases, both the combustion intensity and the peak temperature that can be reached by the electrolyte and carbonate solvent decrease, and the heating rate also slows down, as shown in Fig. 5 . At 100 kPa, the temperature at which the electrolyte is extinguished is 189 °C, which is close to the temperature of 198 °C when the EC/DMC-LiPF6 solvent is extinguished in the literature [27]. According to the research of Fu [59], the massive thermal decomposition of LiPF6 occurs at 190 °C. Since stage 2 is at a late stage of combustion, LiPF6 reaches a suitable temperature for thermal decomposition as the solution temperature increases. And the flame retardant effect of LiPF6 decomposition products is one of the reasons why the electrolyte cannot sustain stage 2.

Fig. 5 shows the peak temperature of the electrolyte is much lower than that of the carbonate mixed solvent. In the electrolyte, again due to the presence of LiPF6, the flame retardant additives do not have a significant effect on reducing the peak temperature. The maximum cooling of the electrolyte occurred in TCPP at 100 kPa, and the peak temperature of the electrolyte decreased by 44 °C. The influence of pressure on the peak temperature of the electrolyte containing TCPP is much less than that of the other three electrolytes, and there is little difference among its peak temperatures at 100 kPa, 80 kPa, and 60 kPa.

After adding flame retardant additives, the temperature of carbonate mixed solvents decreased more significantly than that of electrolytes. TPP has the maximum temperature drop at 60 kPa, which is 40 °C lower than that without addition. HPCTP has the maximum cooling at 80 kPa, which is 62 °C lower than that without addition, but at 40 kPa, the peak temperature increases by 86 °C because of the combustion of stage 2. TCPP decreases by 94 °C, 88 °C, and 66 °C at 100 kPa, 80 kPa, and 60 kPa, respectively, which is more significant than the other two flame retardants. Similar to the rule in the electrolyte, the peak temperatures at these three pressures are close, indicating that the influence of pressure on the peak temperatures of TCPP is much less than that of the other three carbonate mixed solvents. Zhang [62] pointed out that the organic electrolyte also plays a positive role in slowing down the self-heating heating rate of the battery in the early stage of thermal runaway by absorbing heat during the vaporization process. From this point of view, the lower temperature of the electrolyte containing TCPP under atmospheric pressure is more beneficial to the safety of LIBs.

Fig. 6 shows the flame of the electrolytes and carbonate mixed solvents in the stable combustion stage under different pressures. One of the obvious phenomena is the carbonate mixed solvent of stage 1 and electrolyte as the pressure decreases, their flame height increases. This is because, under low pressure, the air density is small, and the combustible liquid surface contacts with less oxygen absolute content. To more fully burn, combustible vapor needs to spread upward, expand the combustion area to make contact with more air, and low pressure is also conducive to the flame and combustible vapor's upward diffusion. The flame height reductions in stage 1 of the carbonate mixed solvents by the flame retardant additives are more pronounced under higher pressures, and the effect on the electrolyte flame height is smaller overall.

Additionally, as the pressure decreases, the brightness of the flame decreases, the yellow part of the flame decreases and the blue part increases. This is mainly related to the volume fraction of carbon soot particles, lowering the pressure directly affects the air density, so that the flame of carbon black generation is reduced. Pressure is proportional to the quadratic absorption coefficient of carbon soot, and carbon black radiation emits yellow light, making the flame more yellow and brighter under atmospheric pressure. While under low pressure, buoyancy-driven oxygen flow is weakened, hindering the generation of carbon black, the main product is cyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, cyclic aromatic hydrocarbon radiation appears light blue, so as the pressure decreases, the flame is bluer. Comparing the electrolyte and carbonate mixed solvent stage 1, which are both controlled by the low-boiling point carbonate for combustion, it is found that the flame color of the electrolyte is significantly more yellow and brighter, indicating that the flame color reaction of LiPF6 changes the flame color of the carbonate solvent. The flame retardant additive has no significant effect on the flame color of the electrolyte containing LiPF6. For the carbonate mixed solvent with additive, the yellow part of the flame increases in stage 2, and the effect of TPP and TCPP is more significant. It can be roughly judged by the flame color that the flame retardant additives increase the flame radiation of stage 2.

In this paper, the effects of adding three flame retardant additives under different pressures on the fire hazards of electrolytes and carbonate mixed solvents were experimentally studied, and the differences in their flame-retardant effects were compared. The major conclusions are summarized as follows.
1.
The stable combustion stage of carbonate mixed solvent can be divided into the low-boiling point carbonate control stage and the high-boiling point carbonate control stage. Lower pressure reduces the MLR and the difference in MLR between the two stages, and the risk of high-boiling point carbonate increases relatively.
2.
There is a critical pressure between 40 kPa and 60 kPa, if it is below the critical pressure, the high-boiling point carbonate control stage will not be able to maintain combustion, and the safety of the carbonate mixed solvent will be increased.
3.
The flame retardant effect of flame retardant additives is mainly reflected in the low-boiling point carbonate control stage, while combustion is promoted in the high-boiling carbonate control stage. The promotion effect is more significant under low pressure, which also leads to the combustion of high-boiling point carbonate in the carbonate mixed solvent containing HPCTP at 40 kPa, increasing the fire risk of the carbonate mixed solvent under low pressure.
4.
The reduction of MLR and combustion time of the three flame retardant additives on the carbonate mixed solvents decrease with the decreasing pressure. Overall, HPCTP performs better in the above-mentioned effects among the three flame retardant additives, with a maximum reduction of 77 s in combustion time and 15.4% in average MLR. TCPP shows the most significant temperature reduction in the carbonate mixed solvent with a maximum drop of 94.5 °C and is less affected by pressure.
5.
The electrolyte is affected by the flame retardant effect of LiPF6 and can only complete the combustion of the low-boiling point carbonate control stage, roughly 2/3 of the electrolyte. The flame retardant effect of the flame retardant is also not as obvious as in the carbonate mixed solvent. The weakening effect of flame retardant addition on electrolyte combustion mass, MLR, and combustion time also decreases with decreasing pressure. The reduction in electrolyte combustion time and electrolyte temperature by TCPP is more significant, with a maximum reduction of 49 s in combustion time and 43.8 °C in electrolyte temperature.

We anticipate our work to provide some future guidance on the safer design of electrolyte in particular in the low ambient pressure. For example, first, providing references for flame retardant additive selection under low pressure. Second, avoiding some phosphonitrile-based flame retardant additives that cannot play a flame retardant role below 40 kPa. Third, optimizing high-boiling carbonate that has a greater risk under low pressure. Fourth, developing other lithium salts with better flame retardant effect. On the other hand, the research on combustion characteristics of the electrolyte containing flame retardant additives under low pressure environment are expanded. We believe that the application of the conclusions can reduce the fire hazards in the use of LIBs.
Qiurui Huang: Writing - Original draft, Data curation, Formal analysis.
Jingwen Weng: Writing - Review & Editing, Conceptualization.
Dongxu Ouyang: Investigation, Resources.
Mingyi Chen: Investigation, Resources.
Xuehui Wang: Project administration.
Jian Wang: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the Basic and Applied Basic Research Fund of Guangdong Province (2021B1515130008) and Key R & D Program of Yunnan Province (202003AC100001).
